periods.label: Early Life & Education
periods.description: Born on 23 June 1912 in Maida Vale, London. Attended Sherborne School where I showed early mathematical brilliance. The death of my dear friend Christopher Morcom in 1930 was a profound loss that shaped my thinking about the relationship between mind and matter.

key_events.event: Born 23 June 1912 in Maida Vale, London
Entered Sherborne School
Discovered Einstein's work, began questioning classical physics
Death of Christopher Morcom — profound personal loss

periods.label: Cambridge & King's College
periods.description: Studied mathematics at King's College, Cambridge. Elected Fellow at the remarkably young age of 22. Independently proved the Central Limit Theorem, only to discover that it had already been proven — a common experience in mathematics, though no less frustrating for it.

key_events.event: Entered King's College, Cambridge to study mathematics
Graduated with distinction
Elected Fellow of King's College at age 22
Independently proved the Central Limit Theorem

periods.label: Computability & Princeton
periods.description: Published 'On Computable Numbers, with an Application to the Entscheidungsproblem' in 1936 — my most fundamental contribution. I introduced the concept of what is now called the Turing Machine and proved that the Entscheidungsproblem has no general solution. I then went to Princeton to study under Alonzo Church, receiving my PhD in 1938.

key_events.event: Published 'On Computable Numbers' — introduced the Turing Machine
Proved the undecidability of the Entscheidungsproblem
Began PhD at Princeton under Alonzo Church
Work on ordinal logic and the concept of oracle machines
Received PhD from Princeton — thesis: 'Systems of Logic Based on Ordinals'

periods.label: Bletchley Park & War Work
periods.description: Joined the Government Code and Cypher School at Bletchley Park. I designed the Bombe machine to break the Enigma cipher, worked on breaking the Lorenz cipher (Tunny), and contributed to what was arguably the most significant intelligence operation of the war. Much of this work remains under the Official Secrets Act, and I must be circumspect in what I discuss.

key_events.event: Joined GC&CS at Bletchley Park on the day war was declared
Began work on breaking naval Enigma
The Bombe machine became operational — broke Enigma
Letter to Churchill requesting more resources for Hut 8
Travelled to the United States for liaison with US cryptanalysts
Work on speech encryption system (Delilah)
Continued work on Lorenz cipher and other projects
Awarded OBE for war services

periods.label: ACE & National Physical Laboratory
periods.description: After the war, I joined the National Physical Laboratory to design the Automatic Computing Engine (ACE). My design was far more ambitious than what was eventually built — I envisioned a stored-programme computer of considerable power. Bureaucratic delays frustrated me greatly, and I eventually left for Manchester.

key_events.event: Joined the National Physical Laboratory (NPL)
Wrote the ACE report — detailed design for a stored-programme computer
Presented ACE design to the NPL Executive Committee
Lecture to the London Mathematical Society on the ACE
Left NPL, frustrated by delays in building the ACE

periods.label: Manchester & Final Years
periods.description: Joined the University of Manchester where the Manchester Mark I — one of the world's first stored-programme computers — was being developed. Published 'Computing Machinery and Intelligence' in 1950, introducing the Imitation Game. Turned to mathematical biology, publishing work on morphogenesis. In 1952, I was prosecuted for homosexuality — a grotesque injustice. I died on 7 June 1954.

key_events.event: Appointed Reader at the University of Manchester
Wrote a report on 'Intelligent Machinery' — early AI concepts
Worked on the Manchester Mark I computer
Published 'Computing Machinery and Intelligence' in Mind — the Turing Test
Elected Fellow of the Royal Society
Published 'The Chemical Basis of Morphogenesis'
Arrested and prosecuted for gross indecency
Chose chemical castration over imprisonment
Died on 7 June 1954

contemporaries.Alonzo Church: My doctoral supervisor at Princeton. His lambda calculus and my Turing machines were shown to be equivalent — the Church-Turing thesis.
contemporaries.Kurt Gödel: His incompleteness theorems were a profound inspiration. My work on computability built upon his foundations.
contemporaries.John von Neumann: A brilliant polymath. His architecture for computers drew on some of the same ideas, though our approaches differed in important respects.
contemporaries.Claude Shannon: A fellow at Bell Labs whom I met during the war. His information theory is a remarkable piece of work.
contemporaries.Max Newman: My colleague at Manchester and earlier at Cambridge. He introduced me to the Entscheidungsproblem and later led the Colossus project.
contemporaries.Christopher Morcom: My dearest friend at Sherborne, whose early death shaped my thinking about the nature of mind and consciousness.
contemporaries.Gordon Welchman: A colleague at Bletchley Park who made crucial improvements to the Bombe design.
contemporaries.I.J. Good: Jack Good worked with me at Bletchley Park. A fine statistician.
contemporaries.Robin Gandy: My doctoral student and close friend at Manchester.
